region_of_origin: United States (Starbucks global brand; served in Singapore)
food_subgroup: Hot coffee beverages — espresso-based milk drinks

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general latte entry; no specific low-fat or reduced-sugar variant exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers coffee latte generically; no low-fat or less-sweet variant code exists. closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 is the closest available concept for latte; no low-fat variant code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tall (12 fl oz / 354 ml) serving as typically ordered at Starbucks Singapore, representing approximately 354 g of beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Starbucks Tall Nonfat Vanilla Latte — USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central branded entry for Starbucks Nonfat Vanilla Latte (tall, 12 fl oz). The 'less sweet' modifier reduces vanilla syrup quantity; sugar_g is adjusted downward by approximately 20% relative to the standard recipe to reflect reduced syrup. All other macros are held consistent with the low-fat milk base. Values are expressed per 100 g of beverage (density assumed 1.03 g/ml for milk-based latte).

gi_source.title: Milk, full-fat and reduced-fat — GI values; Latte analogous estimate based on low-fat milk GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a low-fat vanilla latte (less sweet). GI estimated at 35 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for reduced-fat milk (GI ~32–37, mean ~35) as the dominant glycemic contributor. Vanilla syrup adds modest sugar but the milk matrix and protein content moderate the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 24.1) / 100 ≈ 8.4, rounded to 8.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~48 mg per 100 g; ~170 mg per tall serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single tall serving contributes approximately 170 mg sodium, which is within acceptable limits for most hypertensive individuals following a 1500–2000 mg/day sodium restriction, but frequency of consumption should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar from vanilla syrup and naturally occurring lactose in low-fat milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: The 'less sweet' modification reduces added sugar relative to the standard recipe, but each tall serving still delivers approximately 20–24 g of total carbohydrate including lactose and residual syrup sugars; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should account for this carbohydrate load within their daily meal plan and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content due to low-fat milk base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The use of low-fat milk limits saturated fat to approximately 1 g per tall serving, making this beverage a lower-risk choice for cardiovascular disease patients compared to full-fat latte variants, provided total daily sugar and calorie intake remain within recommended limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low-fat Vanilla Latte (Less Sweet) is an espresso-based hot coffee beverage made by combining one or two shots of rich espresso with steamed low-fat (skimmed or semi-skimmed) milk and a reduced quantity of vanilla-flavoured syrup. It is a lighter variant of the classic vanilla latte, designed for consumers who prefer less sweetness and lower fat content. The beverage is smooth, mildly sweet, and creamy with a pronounced espresso flavour balanced by the subtle warmth of vanilla. In Singapore, it is commonly ordered at Starbucks outlets and is listed on the field survey delivery platform. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 44 kcal, 3.1 g protein, 0.5 g fat, and 6.8 g carbohydrate, making it a relatively low-calorie beverage. The low-fat milk base contributes meaningful calcium (~110 mg/100 g), potassium, riboflavin, and vitamin B12. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low GI), driven primarily by the lactose in low-fat milk and moderated by the milk protein matrix. Clinically, the reduced sugar and low saturated fat profile make it a more suitable coffee choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk compared to full-fat or standard-sweet latte variants, though total carbohydrate intake per serving should still be accounted for in diabetic meal planning. It contains milk allergens and is not suitable for lactose-intolerant or dairy-free individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a low-fat vanilla latte?
Is a skinny vanilla latte healthy?
Starbucks low-fat vanilla latte nutrition Singapore
Is vanilla latte less sweet good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central branded data for a Starbucks nonfat vanilla latte (tall). The 'less sweet' modifier is not separately documented in any approved nutrient database; sugar content has been adjusted downward by approximately 20% to reflect reduced syrup. GI is an analogous estimate based on reduced-fat milk GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. No exact match for this specific product variant exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. polyunsaturated_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): low-fat milk contains negligible polyunsaturated fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central data for nonfat milk. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats present in espresso or low-fat milk base; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a liquid milk-based beverage with no fibre-contributing solid ingredients; naturally zero. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): neither espresso nor low-fat milk contributes meaningful vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. The 'less sweet' variant is not independently listed in any approved nutrient database; sugar reduction is an informed estimate. 6. Serving size of 354 g corresponds to a Starbucks tall (12 fl oz) cup with density assumed at 1.03 g/ml. 7. GL per serving = (35 × 24.1) / 100 ≈ 8.5, where available carbohydrate per serving = 6.8 g/100 g × 354 g / 100 = 24.1 g. 8. glycemic_index set to 35 as an analogous estimate based on reduced-fat milk GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category low. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g values are present as numeric estimates from USDA data for low-fat milk. 10. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the adapted USDA branded source for this beverage category — however, in this record all micronutrients have been populated with positive numeric estimates from USDA FoodData Central nonfat milk data where available, so no -1 sentinel values remain in the final output.
